exercises.title: Client SDK vs Agent SDK, and subagents
exercises.prompt: The Claude Agent SDK is the library form of the Claude Code harness. Explain the difference between the Anthropic Client SDK (raw API) and the Claude Agent SDK (harness shape), and describe subagents (parallelization and context isolation) and when to use them.
exercises.hint: Client SDK: raw API calls (you build the loop). Agent SDK: the full harness (loop, built-in tools, hooks, sessions, trace propagation) — Claude Code as a library. Subagents: separate agent instances for parallelization OR context isolation (a fresh context for a sub-task). Use for parallel work or to keep a sub-task's context out of the main one.
exercises.solution:
**Client SDK vs Agent SDK:**
- **Anthropic Client SDK (raw API):** a thin wrapper over the **raw model API** — you call the model, get a response, and **build the agent loop, tool handling, memory, and everything else yourself** (Lesson 1). It's the low-level building block; you assemble the harness.
- **Claude Agent SDK (harness shape):** the **library form of the Claude Code harness** — it provides the **full agent harness out of the box**: the agent loop, **built-in tools** (file editing, shell, search, etc.), **subagents**, **hooks**, **W3C trace propagation** (Lessons 23-24), and **session-store parity** with Claude Code. Instead of building the harness, you **get Claude Code's proven harness as a library** and customize it. (Claude Managed Agents is the **hosted** alternative for long-running async work — the cloud-runtime version.)
The distinction: **Client SDK = raw API (you build the agent); Agent SDK = the harness (you get a proven agent to customize).** Use the Client SDK when you want full control to build a custom agent; use the Agent SDK when you want **Claude Code's harness shape** (its tools, loop, session handling) as a starting point.

**Subagents (parallelization and context isolation):** subagents are **separate agent instances** the main agent can spawn, serving two purposes:
1. **Parallelization:** spawn **multiple subagents to work on independent subtasks concurrently** (Lesson 3's parallelism) — e.g. search three areas of a codebase at once, then combine. Each subagent runs its own loop in parallel, and results are gathered. Speeds up fan-out work.
2. **Context isolation:** spawn a subagent to handle a **sub-task in a *fresh, isolated context*** — the subagent does its work (which might involve a lot of intermediate reasoning/tool output) in its **own context window**, and returns **only the result** to the main agent. This **keeps the sub-task's noisy intermediate context out of the main agent's context** — the main agent's context stays clean (it gets the distilled result, not all the sub-task's steps). Critical for **managing the context budget** (Phase 11.5, Lesson 7) — a research-heavy sub-task can burn thousands of tokens of intermediate context that the main agent doesn't need; isolating it in a subagent keeps the main context focused.
**When to use subagents:** 
- **Parallelization:** when you have **independent subtasks** that can run concurrently (fan-out work) — speed.
- **Context isolation:** when a **sub-task generates a lot of intermediate context** the main agent doesn't need — spawn a subagent so the main context stays clean and the main agent gets only the result. This is a key **context-management** technique (it's why Claude Code uses subagents for big searches/explorations — keep the main context lean by isolating noisy sub-tasks). Use subagents to **parallelize independent work and to isolate context-heavy sub-tasks**, keeping the main agent fast and focused.

exercises.title: The session store and trace propagation
exercises.prompt: Name the Claude Agent SDK Python session-store surface (append, load, list_sessions, delete, list_subkeys) and the role of --session-mirror, and explain W3C trace propagation. Why does session-store parity with Claude Code matter?
exercises.hint: Session store: append (add to a session), load (read it), list_sessions, delete, list_subkeys — durable session state. --session-mirror: mirror sessions for parity/inspection. W3C trace propagation: pass trace context (traceparent) across calls/subagents so one trace spans everything. Parity matters so SDK agents and Claude Code share session state/format.
exercises.solution:
**The Python session-store surface:** the Claude Agent SDK provides a **durable session store** for agent state across turns/sessions (repo memory, Lesson 34):
- **`append`:** **add to a session** — write new state/messages/events to a session (the agent records its work).
- **`load`:** **read a session** — retrieve the stored session state (the agent reads its prior state at the start of a turn/session).
- **`list_sessions`:** **enumerate sessions** — see all stored sessions (for management, handoff, inspection).
- **`delete`:** **remove a session** — clean up.
- **`list_subkeys`:** **enumerate the sub-keys** within a session — structured access to the session's contents.
This surface lets the agent **persist and reload durable session state** — the foundation for **memory across turns and sessions** (Lessons 7-8, 34, 40's handoff). State lives in the session store (durable), not just the volatile chat context.

**`--session-mirror`:** **mirrors sessions** — keeps a parallel copy/reflection of session state (for **parity, inspection, or syncing** between the SDK and Claude Code, or for observability). It ensures session state is **visible and consistent** across the SDK and the Claude Code harness — so you can inspect what an SDK agent did using the same session format/tools as Claude Code.

**W3C trace propagation:** the SDK uses **W3C trace context** (the standard `traceparent` header/format) to **propagate trace context across calls and subagents** — so that when the main agent calls a tool, spawns a subagent, or makes an LLM call, the **trace context flows through**, and **one distributed trace spans the entire agent execution** (main agent → subagents → tools → LLM calls). This is **standardized distributed tracing** (Lessons 23-24, Phase 13.20's OTel GenAI) — using the **W3C standard** means the traces are **interoperable** (any W3C-trace-aware backend can consume them) and the **whole multi-agent, multi-tool execution stitches into one coherent trace**. Without trace propagation, subagent and tool spans would be disconnected; with W3C propagation, you get the **full end-to-end trace** essential for debugging distributed agents.

**Why session-store parity with Claude Code matters:** **parity** means the SDK's session store uses the **same surface, format, and semantics as Claude Code's** — so:
- **Interoperability:** an agent built with the SDK and Claude Code itself **share the same session state/format** — you can **inspect, resume, or hand off** sessions between them. A session created in Claude Code can be loaded by an SDK agent and vice versa.
- **Consistency / no surprises:** the SDK behaves like the proven Claude Code harness (the SDK *is* Claude Code as a library, Exercise 1) — so what you learn/build in one transfers to the other, and the session management is **battle-tested** (Claude Code's real-world-proven approach).
- **Tooling reuse:** the same session-inspection/management tools (`--session-mirror`, etc.) work across both.
- **Production-readiness:** session-store parity means **durable, inspectable, resumable** agent state (Lesson 34's repo memory, Lesson 40's handoff) using the **same proven mechanism** as the flagship harness — you inherit Claude Code's reliability for state management rather than reinventing it.
Parity ensures the SDK isn't a different, untested thing — it's **the same harness, so its session handling (durable state, handoff, inspection) is the proven Claude Code mechanism**, which is exactly what you want for reliable production agents (and it sets up the workbench's repo-memory and handoff patterns, Lessons 34, 40).

exercises.title: The harness-shape philosophy, hosted vs library, and where Claude Agent SDK fits
exercises.prompt: Explain the Claude Agent SDK's 'harness as a library' philosophy (built-in tools, hooks, subagents, session parity), how it differs from lighter SDKs (OpenAI, Lesson 16) and heavier orchestration (LangGraph, Lesson 13), the choice between the library SDK and Claude Managed Agents (hosted), and when this shape is the right fit.
exercises.hint: Harness-as-library: you get the proven Claude Code harness (tools, hooks, subagents, sessions) not just primitives. vs OpenAI SDK (lighter, fewer built-ins) and LangGraph (lower-level state graph, you build the harness). Library = self-hosted control; Managed Agents = hosted for long-running async. Fits Claude-Code-style coding/agentic work.
exercises.solution:
**The 'harness as a library' philosophy:** the Claude Agent SDK's distinctive idea is that it ships the **entire proven Claude Code harness as a library** — not just primitives you assemble, but a **complete, battle-tested agent harness** with:
- **Built-in tools** (file editing, shell execution, search, etc.) — the agent can *do* real work out of the box (vs assembling tools yourself).
- **Subagents** (parallelization + context isolation, Exercise 1).
- **Hooks** (intercept/customize the agent's behavior at defined points).
- **Session store with Claude Code parity** (durable, inspectable, resumable state, Exercise 2).
- **W3C trace propagation** (full distributed observability, Exercise 2).
The philosophy: **don't make developers rebuild the harness — give them Claude Code's proven one as a customizable library.** You get a **working, reliable agent** (the same shape that powers Claude Code) and **customize it**, rather than building the loop/tools/memory/observability from scratch. It's the opposite of 'minimal primitives' — it's 'a complete, proven harness you adapt.'

**How it differs from lighter SDKs and heavier orchestration:**
- **vs OpenAI Agents SDK (lighter, Lesson 16):** the OpenAI SDK is **minimal primitives** (Agent/Handoff/Guardrail/Session/Tracing) — lightweight, fewer built-ins, you add tools. The Claude Agent SDK is **a fuller harness** — **built-in tools, subagents, hooks, the Claude Code shape** — more 'batteries included.' OpenAI SDK = lightweight provider layer; Claude Agent SDK = a complete proven agent harness. The Claude SDK does more out of the box (especially for **coding/agentic work**, since it's Claude Code).
- **vs LangGraph (lower-level, Lesson 13):** LangGraph is **low-level stateful orchestration** — you **build the agent's structure** as an explicit state graph (nodes/edges/state). The Claude Agent SDK gives you a **ready-made harness** (the agent is already built, Claude-Code-style) — higher-level, less assembly, but **less custom control over the orchestration structure**. LangGraph for **custom durable state machines**; Claude Agent SDK for **the proven Claude Code agent shape, customized**.
So the Claude Agent SDK sits at **'complete proven harness, customizable'** — more than minimal primitives (OpenAI), less DIY than building a state graph (LangGraph). Its sweet spot is **getting a reliable Claude-Code-style agent quickly** (Phase 11.17's match-the-abstraction).

**Library SDK vs Claude Managed Agents (hosted):**
- **Library SDK (self-hosted):** you **run the agent yourself** (in your infra), with **full control** over the environment, tools, and execution. Use when you want **control, custom tools/environment, or to run in your own infrastructure** (data residency, custom integration).
- **Claude Managed Agents (hosted):** Anthropic **hosts and runs the agent** for you — the managed alternative, especially for **long-running async work** (background tasks that run for minutes/hours, Phase 13.13, Lesson 29's queue/background runtimes). Use when you want **zero ops, hosted reliability, and long-running async execution** without managing the runtime yourself.
The choice is the recurring **self-hosted (control) vs managed (convenience)** axis (Phase 13.17/18/21): **library SDK for control/custom/self-hosted; Managed Agents for hosted, long-running, zero-ops async work.**

**When the Claude Agent SDK shape is the right fit:** choose it when:
- **You want a Claude-Code-style agentic/coding agent** — the SDK *is* Claude Code as a library, so it's ideal for **coding, file/repo manipulation, shell-driven, tool-heavy agentic work** (its built-in tools and subagents are tuned for this).
- **You want a proven harness, not to build one** — you value **getting a reliable agent quickly** (built-in tools, sessions, subagents, observability) over assembling primitives or designing a state graph.
- **You need context isolation / parallelization** via subagents (context-heavy or fan-out work, Exercise 1).
- **You want session parity and W3C tracing out of the box** (durable state, full observability without setup).
- **You're on the Anthropic/Claude stack** and want first-class integration (and the option of Managed Agents for hosted long-running work).
**Reach for something else when:** you need a **custom durable state machine** (→ LangGraph), a **minimal provider-aligned layer** on OpenAI (→ OpenAI Agents SDK), **concurrent fault-isolated actors** (→ AutoGen), or **provider portability/role-based crews** (→ routing layer / CrewAI). The Claude Agent SDK is the right fit when you want **the proven Claude Code harness as a customizable library** — especially for **coding and tool-heavy agentic work** — trading some orchestration flexibility for a complete, reliable, batteries-included agent. As always (Phase 11.17), **match the framework's shape to your problem**: Claude Agent SDK for the Claude-Code-style agent shape, with the library/hosted choice driven by your control-vs-convenience needs.
